exercises.title: Date Formatting and Parsing
exercises.description: Practice datetime formatting with strftime and parsing with strptime. Convert between different date formats and extract date components.
exercises.starterCode:
from datetime import datetime, date

# Fixed date for deterministic output
dt = datetime(2024, 3, 15, 14, 30, 45)

# TODO: Format dt in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD)
# Format: "ISO: 2024-03-15"

# TODO: Format dt as US style (March 15, 2024)
# Format: "US: March 15, 2024"

# TODO: Format dt with time (15/03/2024 14:30)
# Format: "EU: 15/03/2024 14:30"

# TODO: Format dt as weekday name + date
# Format: "Weekday: Friday, March 15"

# TODO: Parse this string back to a datetime object
date_str = "2024-12-25 09:00:00"
# Use strptime with format "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"
# Then print: "Christmas 2024 is on a Wednesday"

# TODO: Calculate days until Christmas from dt
# Print: "Days until Christmas: 285"
exercises.expectedOutput:
ISO: 2024-03-15
US: March 15, 2024
EU: 15/03/2024 14:30
Weekday: Friday, March 15
Christmas 2024 is on a Wednesday
Days until Christmas: 285

exercises.title: timedelta Arithmetic
exercises.description: Use timedelta to calculate durations, add/subtract from dates, and find working days. Practice date comparison and sorting.
exercises.starterCode:
from datetime import datetime, date, timedelta

# Fixed base date
base = datetime(2024, 1, 15, 9, 0, 0)

# TODO: Calculate dates for: tomorrow, next_week, in_30_days, last_month
# Print each:
# "Tomorrow: 2024-01-16"
# "Next week: 2024-01-22"
# "In 30 days: 2024-02-14"
# "Last month: 2023-12-15"

print()

# TODO: Calculate the duration between two events
start = datetime(2024, 3, 1, 8, 0, 0)
end = datetime(2024, 3, 15, 17, 30, 0)
diff = end - start
# Print: "Duration: 14 days"
# Print: "Duration in hours: 345.5 hours"

print()

# TODO: Count working days (Mon-Fri) between two dates
def count_working_days(start_date, end_date):
    # TODO: Iterate from start to end, count days where weekday() < 5
    pass

work_start = date(2024, 1, 1)
work_end = date(2024, 1, 31)
print(f"Working days in January 2024: {count_working_days(work_start, work_end)}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Tomorrow: 2024-01-16
Next week: 2024-01-22
In 30 days: 2024-02-14
Last month: 2023-12-15

Duration: 14 days
Duration in hours: 345.5 hours

Working days in January 2024: 23
hints: count = sum(1 for i in range((end_date - start_date).days + 1) if (start_date + timedelta(days=i)).weekday() < 5)

exercises.title: Timezone-Aware Datetimes
exercises.description: Work with timezone-aware datetimes using timezone objects. Convert between UTC and different fixed offsets, and format for display.
exercises.starterCode:
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta

# Create timezone objects for major cities (fixed offsets)
utc = timezone.utc
new_york = timezone(timedelta(hours=-5))   # EST
london = timezone(timedelta(hours=0))       # GMT (same as UTC in winter)
tokyo = timezone(timedelta(hours=9))        # JST
sydney = timezone(timedelta(hours=11))      # AEDT

# Fixed UTC time for deterministic output
utc_time = datetime(2024, 3, 15, 14, 0, 0, tzinfo=utc)

# TODO: Convert utc_time to each timezone and print
# Format: "UTC:      2024-03-15 14:00 +00:00"
# Format: "New York: 2024-03-15 09:00 -05:00"
# Format: "London:   2024-03-15 14:00 +00:00"
# Format: "Tokyo:    2024-03-16 23:00 +09:00"
# Format: "Sydney:   2024-03-16 01:00 +11:00"
hints: # %z gives +0000 format; to get +00:00 use manual formatting or isoformat()
offset = dt.utcoffset(); hours = int(offset.total_seconds() // 3600); sign = '+' if hours >= 0 else '-'

warmup.title: Quick Recall: datetime Components
warmup.description: What does this code print? Trace the datetime component access.
warmup.starterCode:
from datetime import datetime

dt = datetime(2024, 6, 15, 9, 30, 0)
print(dt.year)
print(dt.month)
print(dt.weekday())  # 0=Monday
print(dt.strftime('%A'))
